rationale: Added ADX regime filter (adx > 20) to skip ranging markets. EMA crossover alone whipsaws in choppy conditions — ADX confirms a trend is actually in place before entering.
Raised ADX threshold from 20 to 25. Technically higher fitness but hollow — 4 of 5 windows have fewer than 3 trades. ADX 25 is too restrictive for 1h crypto data.
note: rejected_qualitative: too few trades across windows despite numeric improvement

rationale: Replaced EMA crossover with MACD histogram zero-cross + ADX. MACD histogram crosses zero much more frequently than EMA crossovers, generating excessive trades that the ADX filter cannot compensate for.
Added EMA50 macro trend filter — only enter when close > EMA50. Keeps strategy out of downtrends at the hourly level. W3 improved from +1.74 to +2.12 Sharpe.
Raised rsi_entry_min 50→55. Cut a key W2 entry, net worse.
Added EMA50 trend breakdown as exit signal. In choppy markets this triggers on every dip below EMA50, causing exits at lows and dramatically worsening W1.
Require RSI to be rising at entry (rsi > rsi.shift(1)) — demands accelerating momentum, not just positive momentum. Reduces whipsaw entries from stalling EMA crossovers.

Add ADX trend-fading exit: exit position when ADX drops below threshold (adx < 20). Cuts losses before full EMA crossover reversal by detecting when the trend is weakening. Dramatically improves W1 (-1.037→-0.094) and W4 (-0.240→+0.669).

Add 2-bar hysteresis to ADX exit: require ADX < threshold for 2 consecutive bars before exiting. Prevents premature exit on temporary ADX dips in strong trend windows. Recovers W3 (+0.486→+1.026), W4 (+0.669→+1.175), W5 (+0.292→+0.702).

Raise rsi_entry_min from 50 to 55. Requires stronger momentum at entry. Reduces W1 Sharpe from -0.752 to -0.390, cuts std from 1.251 to 0.663. Mean drops but variance reduction dominates in fitness formula.

W1 is the clear outlier with Sharpe=-0.390 and only 29% win rate on 7 trades, dragging down mean and inflating variance. The current rsi_entry_min=55 combined with adx_threshold=20 is still letting in low-quality entries in choppy/ranging conditions (W1 is a sideways-to-down market: Mar-Jun 2024). Raising adx_threshold from 20 to 25 was previously rejected in isolation, but the strategy has since added the RSI-rising filter and 2-bar ADX hysteresis exit. These structural improvements mean a stricter ADX entry threshold (25) is now complementary — it should filter out more of the false breakouts in W1 without sacrificing the few high-quality setups in W2-W5 which had ADX well above 25.

W1 (Sharpe=-0.390, 7 trades, 29% win rate) is the clear drag — too many low-quality trades are being taken in what appears to be a choppy/ranging market. The last rejected attempt raised rsi_entry_min too aggressively. Instead, I'll raise the ADX threshold from 20 to 25 to demand stronger trend confirmation before entering, while simultaneously raising ema_fast to 15 and ema_slow to 30 to slow the crossover signal and reduce whipsaws. The slightly slower EMA pair should reduce the 7-trade over-trading in W1 without eliminating the 3-trade setups in W2-W4 which are already well-spaced, improving both mean and variance of Sharpe.

W1 is the clear outlier (Sharpe=-0.390, 7 trades, 29% win rate) while other windows have decent Sharpe. The high trade count in W1 suggests whipsaw entries in a choppy/ranging market. I'll add a Bollinger Band width filter as a volatility regime gate — only enter when BB width is above its rolling median (expanding volatility favors trend-following), which should suppress false EMA crossovers in low-volatility, ranging conditions that plagued W1. This directly addresses the variance penalty without sacrificing the winning windows which had fewer, higher-quality trades.

W1 has 7 trades with only 29% win rate — the strategy is over-trading in a choppy/ranging regime. The fix is to add a Bollinger Band width filter that prevents entries when volatility is compressed (tight bands = no trend), and to tighten the RSI rising requirement by also requiring RSI > 50 (not just rising). This adds a "breakout from compression" pre-condition that naturally reduces false crossover signals in sideways markets while preserving entries in genuine trending regimes (W2–W4).

W1 is the persistent outlier (Sharpe=-0.390, 7 trades, 29% win rate) while other windows average ~1.1 Sharpe with 3 trades each. The core problem in W1 is over-trading — 7 trades vs 3 in healthy windows suggests the ADX/RSI filters aren't tight enough to block poor-quality entries in that regime. Raising the ADX threshold from 20→25 was rejected (#2) in isolation, but combining a modest ADX raise (20→22) with a tighter RSI entry floor (55→58) should reduce W1's false entries without over-filtering the profitable windows that already use very few trades.

W1 (2024-03-29 → 2024-06-28) is the clear drag with Sharpe=-0.390, 7 trades, 29% win rate. This looks like a choppy/sideways period where the strategy generates too many false entries. The last 5 attempts to fix W1 all failed (experiments 11-15), suggesting that parametric tweaks and additional filters are too aggressive and kill good trades in other windows. Instead, I'll add a simple ATR-based trailing stop exit to replace the broad trend_fading exit. This should cut losses faster in W1 (improving its Sharpe) while letting winners run in trending windows. The trailing stop exits when price drops more than atr_stop_mult × ATR from its recent high, which adapts to each regime's volatility naturally.

W1 (2024-03-29 → 2024-06-28) is the persistent drag with Sharpe=-0.390, 7 trades, 29% win rate. Many attempts to fix W1 have failed by breaking other windows. After 15 failed attempts, all focused on fixing W1, it's clear that tweaking the existing EMA crossover framework isn't working. The core problem is that EMA crossover generates too many false signals in choppy/sideways markets (W1). Instead of trying to filter those out, I'm switching to a fundamentally different approach: a mean-reversion strategy using Bollinger Bands with RSI confirmation and ATR-based trailing exits. This should perform more consistently across regimes because: (1) it catches dips in bull markets, (2) it catches bounces in bear/sideways markets, and (3) ATR-based stops adapt to volatility regime. The key insight is to use a wider Bollinger Band (2.2 std) with RSI oversold confirmation for entries, and exit when price returns to the middle band or hits an ATR stop — this naturally adapts to different volatility regimes.
